evals.prompt: Change the appearance of counted barcodes in my MatrixScan Count app: give recognized barcodes a green background and not-in-list barcodes an orange background. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: Because the view uses the default Icon style, the skill customizes the icon background per state via the BarcodeCountViewDelegate: iconForRecognizedBarcode returns a BarcodeCountIcon with a green background and iconForRecognizedBarcodeNotInList an orange one (built with ScanditIconBuilder().withBackgroundColor(...).build()). It does NOT switch to the Dot style or use brush properties. The rest of the integration is unchanged.

assertions.text: barcodeCountView.delegate is set and the controller conforms to BarcodeCountViewDelegate
iconForRecognizedBarcode returns a BarcodeCountIcon with a green background (ScanditIconBuilder().withBackgroundColor(green)...build() wrapped in BarcodeCountIcon(defaultIcon:accessibleIcon:))
iconForRecognizedBarcodeNotInList returns a BarcodeCountIcon with an orange background
It customizes the ICON (the default style is Icon) — it does NOT switch to .dot or set recognizedBrush/notInListBrush
Any ScanditIconType used is a real case (e.g. .checkmark) — not guessed; the context, mode, camera, listener, uiDelegate and lifecycle are unchanged

evals.prompt: In my MatrixScan Count app, change the background color of each counted barcode based on its data: purple if the data is an even number, green if odd, and blue if it can't be parsed as a number. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: On the default Icon style, the skill implements BarcodeCountViewDelegate.iconForRecognizedBarcode, parsing trackedBarcode.barcode.data as an Int and returning a BarcodeCountIcon whose background is purple (even), green (odd), or blue (unparseable), built via ScanditIconBuilder().withBackgroundColor(...). The optional barcode.data is unwrapped before parsing. Style stays Icon.

assertions.text: barcodeCountView.delegate is set; the controller implements iconForRecognizedBarcode returning BarcodeCountIcon?
trackedBarcode.barcode.data is UNWRAPPED (it is String?) before Int parsing (e.g. guard let / if let / Int(data ?? ""))
Returns a BarcodeCountIcon with a purple background for even, green for odd, blue for nil/unparseable (ScanditIconBuilder().withBackgroundColor(...).build())
It customizes icons (default Icon style) — does NOT switch to .dot or use brushes
No icon API is mis-used (BarcodeCountIcon(defaultIcon:accessibleIcon:), real ScanditIconType if any); rest of integration unchanged

evals.prompt: In my SwiftUI MatrixScan Count app, change the background color of each counted barcode based on its data: purple if even, green if odd, and blue if it can't be parsed as a number. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountSwiftUIView.swift
evals.expected_output: Same as eval 2 but inside the UIKit CountViewController hosted in the SwiftUI file: iconForRecognizedBarcode returns a BarcodeCountIcon with the even/odd/unparseable background color (Icon style). The SwiftUI ScanView struct and the representable stay free of Scandit code.

assertions.text: Inside the UIKit CountViewController, barcodeCountView.delegate is set and iconForRecognizedBarcode is implemented returning BarcodeCountIcon?
barcode.data is unwrapped before Int parsing; returns BarcodeCountIcon with purple(even)/green(odd)/blue(unparseable) background via ScanditIconBuilder
Customizes the icon (default Icon style); does NOT switch to .dot or use brushes
The change is in the UIKit layer; the SwiftUI ScanView struct and the UIViewControllerRepresentable remain free of Scandit API calls
Rest of the integration unchanged

evals.prompt: Switch my MatrixScan Count view to the Dot style and make the counted-barcode dots green. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: The skill creates the BarcodeCountView with style: .dot and sets the dot color via a Brush — barcodeCountView.recognizedBrush = Brush(fill: green, ...) (or the brushForRecognizedBarcode delegate). This is the explicit brush/Dot path. The rest of the integration is unchanged.

assertions.text: The BarcodeCountView is created with style: .dot (the style: initializer argument)
The recognized dot color is set to green via a Brush — recognizedBrush = Brush(fill:stroke:strokeWidth:) (or brushForRecognizedBarcode returning such a Brush)
Brush is constructed with fill:/stroke:/strokeWidth: (NOT fillColor:/strokeColor:)
It uses the brush path (since the user explicitly asked for the Dot style) — not icon customization
The context, mode, camera, listener, uiDelegate and lifecycle are unchanged

evals.prompt: I set barcodeCountView.recognizedBrush to a custom color in my MatrixScan Count app, but the highlight color doesn't change. Why?
evals.expected_output: The skill explains that brushes only render in the Dot style; the default Icon style ignores recognizedBrush. Two fixes: either create the view with style: .dot to use brushes, OR (keeping the Icon style) customize the icon via iconForRecognizedBarcode (e.g. its background color).

assertions.text: The answer explains brushes (recognizedBrush / brushForRecognizedBarcode) only take effect in the Dot style, and the default is the Icon style
It gives the Dot fix: create the BarcodeCountView with style: .dot
It also notes that on the Icon style you customize the icon instead (iconForRecognizedBarcode — e.g. background color), rather than a brush
It does not invent APIs (no recognizedIcon property; real ScanditIconType names)

evals.prompt: In MatrixScan Count, what are the ways to customize the appearance of a specific counted barcode?
evals.expected_output: The skill describes both mechanisms via the BarcodeCountViewDelegate: for the Icon style, iconForRecognizedBarcode (per barcode) and the imperative setIcon(_:forRecognizedBarcode:) returning a BarcodeCountIcon; for the Dot style, brushForRecognizedBarcode (per barcode) and setBrush(_:forRecognizedBarcode:) returning a Brush. It notes which applies to which style.

assertions.text: It covers the ICON path (Icon style): iconForRecognizedBarcode delegate callback and/or setIcon(_:forRecognizedBarcode:) returning a BarcodeCountIcon
It covers the BRUSH path (Dot style): brushForRecognizedBarcode delegate callback and/or setBrush(_:forRecognizedBarcode:) returning a Brush
It correctly ties each to its style (icons → Icon/default, brushes → Dot)
It does NOT use the non-existent brushForUnrecognizedBarcode name or a recognizedIcon property

evals.prompt: In my MatrixScan Count app, keep the default look for counted barcodes but make the ones whose data starts with "X" stand out with a red background. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: On the default Icon style, the skill implements iconForRecognizedBarcode, returning a BarcodeCountIcon with a red background when trackedBarcode.barcode.data starts with "X" and nil (keep the default icon) otherwise. The optional data is unwrapped. Style stays Icon.

assertions.text: barcodeCountView.delegate is set; iconForRecognizedBarcode is implemented returning BarcodeCountIcon?
Returns a red-background BarcodeCountIcon (ScanditIconBuilder().withBackgroundColor(red)) when trackedBarcode.barcode.data unwraps and hasPrefix("X")
Returns nil (keep the SDK default icon) for all other recognized barcodes
It customizes the icon (default Icon style); does NOT switch to .dot or use brushes
barcode.data is unwrapped (String?); the rest of the integration is unchanged

evals.prompt: In my MatrixScan Count app, show a custom icon on every counted barcode — a white check mark on a green circle. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: The skill sets barcodeCountView.delegate and implements iconForRecognizedBarcode, returning the same BarcodeCountIcon for every barcode — built via ScanditIconBuilder().withIcon(.checkmark).withIconColor(.white).withBackgroundColor(green).withBackgroundShape(.circle).build() wrapped in BarcodeCountIcon(defaultIcon:accessibleIcon:). The default Icon style is kept. The rest of the integration is unchanged.

assertions.text: barcodeCountView.delegate is set; the controller implements iconForRecognizedBarcode returning BarcodeCountIcon?
It returns the SAME icon for every recognized barcode (one icon for all — not per-barcode branching), since there is no per-state icon property

The icon uses ScanditIconBuilder with the REAL check-mark case .checkmark (NOT .check), white icon color, green background, .circle background shape, wrapped in BarcodeCountIcon(defaultIcon:accessibleIcon:)
It keeps the default Icon style (does not switch to .dot) and does not invent a per-state icon property
The context, mode, camera, listener, uiDelegate and lifecycle are unchanged

evals.prompt: In my MatrixScan Count app, after a barcode is scanned I want to show a per-barcode status overlay — mark items I know are expired as expired. File: IntegratedBarcodeCountViewController.swift
evals.expected_output: The skill enables status mode: sets barcodeCountView.shouldShowStatusIconsOnScan = true, calls barcodeCountView.setStatusProvider(self), and makes the controller conform to BarcodeCountStatusProvider implementing statusRequested(for:callback:). The provider builds a BarcodeCountStatusItem per TrackedBarcode (with BarcodeCountStatus, e.g. .expired) and returns them via a BarcodeCountStatusSuccessResult passed to callback.onStatusReady(_:). The rest of the integration is unchanged.

assertions.text: barcodeCountView.shouldShowStatusIconsOnScan = true is set (or barcodeCountView.shouldShowStatusModeButton = true) to surface status mode
barcodeCountView.setStatusProvider(self) is called
The controller conforms to BarcodeCountStatusProvider and implements statusRequested(for:callback:) with a [TrackedBarcode] parameter and a BarcodeCountStatusProviderCallback
A BarcodeCountStatusItem is built per barcode via BarcodeCountStatusItem(barcode:status:) using a real BarcodeCountStatus case such as .expired
The statuses are returned via BarcodeCountStatusSuccessResult(statusList:statusModeEnabledMessage:statusModeDisabledMessage:) passed to callback.onStatusReady(_:)
It does not invent APIs (no per-barcode status property on the view; real BarcodeCountStatus cases); the context, mode, camera, listener, uiDelegate and lifecycle are unchanged
